text_segment: Cherokee marbles, called Digadayosdi, combines elements of golf and cornhole. Players take turns rolling stones toward a series of five holes. Other players can hit your stone away during their turns.

Active: Players roll stones toward holes while blocking opponents.
Progressive: Players are rolling stones toward holes while blocking opponents.
Passive: Stones are rolled toward holes by players who block opponents.
Subordinate: While aiming for holes, players can block their opponents.

text_segment: Cherokee Indians used weapons including clubs, tomahawks, bows and arrows, and axes. Their culture taught fighting skills at an early age. When heading into battle, warriors wore strips of otter skin to show they were fighters.

text_segment: Cherokee Indians fought bravely to resist European expansion into their territory. Following the Cherokee-American wars, Cherokees were forcibly relocated west of the Mississippi River between 1830 and 1850. This sad event became known as the Trail of Tears.

Active: The government forced Cherokee people to move west.
Progressive: The government was forcing Cherokee people to move west.
Passive: Cherokee people were forced by the government to move west.
Subordinate: After losing their wars, Cherokee people were forced to move west.

text_segment: Snow goggles were introduced to European explorers by Arctic and Subarctic tribes. These goggles protected eyes from snow blindness. Later, the design inspired sunglasses that became popular throughout the world.

text_segment: The lifestyles and practices of American Indian tribes were heavily influenced by their location. In colder regions, tribes needed warm furs to survive harsh winters. In warmer regions like the Southeast, finding warm shelter was less important.

Passive: How tribes lived was shaped by their climate.
